Reasons to continue buying PLTR

There are numerous reasons to continue buying Palantir (PLTR)(PLTR:CA) even though my previous bullish call did not age quite well, the stock is down by 4% since then. Moreover, there were several positive earnings revisions over the last several weeks, which means that long-term forward multiples compressed notably due to stagnating share prices over the same period. Palantir's bullish earnings revisions setup is particularly important given that the company is set to release its Q4 report next week, February 2.

As the above picture shows, over the last three months, EPS forecasts increased by double-digit percentages across the board for several next quarters. Given Palantir's nearly flawless earnings surprise record, I do not think that these new EPS growth targets have become too aggressive. Moreover, history suggests that PLTR's earnings frequently become extremely powerful catalysts that generously reward analysts.

I have analyzed PLTR's share price performance one week after each quarterly earnings release over the last two years, and the setup looks extremely bullish for investors who buy PLTR before earnings. PLTR delivered massive post-earnings rallies in five out of the last eight quarterly earnings releases. The ~63% success rate looks particularly strong given that the remaining three quarters of my sample were with quite mild post-earnings selloffs. As a result, the average post-earnings share price spike over the last two years amounted to 21%. Therefore, Palantir's investors buying before earnings are frequently rewarded.

Some readers might say that the post-Q3 earnings share price movement was negative, which might create adverse momentum into the Q4 earnings season. It might be true, but it is vital to take into account the broader market's sentiment. CNN's Fear and Greed index is a useful tool to assess how the market's sentiment changed historically, and we can see that investors were under "extreme fear" in early November when PLTR released its Q3 earnings. This time the situation is different, as the market is currently moderately greedy. The "Fear and Greed" chart also shows that the setup was approximately the same back in early August when PLTR released its Q2 earnings, which was marked with a 20%+ rally during the week after the report went live. With that being said, robust market sentiment is likely to be a solid share price tailwind this time.

As I mentioned earlier, PLTR's earnings surprise record is strong. Over the last eight quarters, the average EPS surprise was approximately 12%, and the average topline surprise was ~4%. Median surprises are quite close to average numbers, which adds reliability to my approach as the distribution is not heavily skewed by outliers. Wall Street analysts forecast PLTR's Q4 revenue to be $1.34 billion (62% YoY increase), and the normalized EPS to be $0.23 (64% YoY increase). If PLTR's EPS and revenue surprises for Q4 will be approximately in line with historical averages, the company can potentially surprise investors and deliver $1.4 billion in quarterly sales together with $0.26 in normalized EPS. Therefore, it is unlikely to be a one-cent EPS surprise, which decreases the probability of investors being disappointed.

For sure, any earnings release is not only about headline numbers but also about the guidance and the management's comments during the earnings call. To understand what the guidance might be, we need to look at qualitative trends and factors. Palantir is a large beneficiary of the AI software revolution, meaning that we need to look at what surveys of C-level executives of businesses say about their AI spending plans for 2026. BCG is one of the largest consulting companies in the world, and I consider it highly reputable and reliable. According to the fresh BCG AI Radar update, investments in AI software and applications are projected to double in 2026, and business leaders are eager to invest even if these investments do not pay off rapidly. Since Palantir's Commercial segment is the company's main growth engine at the moment, such sentiment from business leaders surveyed by BCG means that the environment remains extremely positive.

Moreover, Palantir's engineers continue to actively work on expanding the number of use cases for the company's Artificial Intelligence Platform (AIP). I think so because Palantir recently expanded its strategic partnership with a South Korean giant, HD Hyundai. According to the official release, the expanded partnership will unlock various use cases for AIP such as electric systems, robotics, and marine aftermarket services. All these areas are quite large markets, and collaborating with a large conglomerate like HD Hyundai certainly adds more validation to the company's AIP. Thus, the momentum of discovering new use cases for Palantir's offerings remains strong, and it is likely to positively contribute to the FY2026 guidance.

Finally, it appears that investors are extremely cautious about Palantir's upcoming earnings release as the 14-day RSI is just above the oversold territory. I believe that is a favorable setup, as it will be much easier for Palantir to impress investors when they are cautious rather than if they were overexcited. Therefore, the technical analysis setup also looks favorable before the upcoming earnings release.

Reasons to avoid PLTR

Palantir's fundamentals remain intact, and the stock has a particularly good habit of soaring post-earnings. However, there is an apparent elephant in the room, which is Palantir's extremely growth-oriented valuation. Just look at these multiples; they are simply sky-high. Such valuation means that investors expect nearly flawless execution over the next several years, which will allow PLTR to deliver exceptional EPS growth.

The combination of facts like businesses planning to double down on their AI spending and Palantir rapidly expanding the number of use cases for AIP will certainly result in a massive P/E ratio compression. However, the biggest problem is that it is impossible to quantify reliably Palantir's long-term EPS growth trajectory to understand what its P/E ratio will look like closer to 2030. Of course, C-level executives of businesses say that they are ready to increase AI investments even if they don't see the positive effect in 2026, but we cannot be confident that they will remain that patient in 2028 or 2029 as well. Therefore, whether Palantir's valuation is fair or not is a highly controversial question.

Moreover, there are too many big uncertainties beyond the AI industry itself. We currently observe a highly complicated geopolitical landscape where international relationships of the U.S. are getting really stress-tested. In case the U.S.'s historically strong political bonds and Europe become weaker, it might adversely affect Palantir's potential to drive international expansion. Thus, the company's revenue growth pace might deteriorate significantly once Palantir's domestic penetration achieves peak levels.

The verdict

Palantir's good habit of demonstrating massive rallies post-earnings is the main reason why I am adding more shares to my portfolio. Both fundamental and technical setups look favorable for PLTR to deliver large earnings and guidance surprises. Palantir's sky-high multiples make investing large amounts quite scary, meaning that I reiterate a plain buy for PLTR.

data.title: IEF: Staying Bullish, Despite The Noise
data.description:
This article provide an updated outlook on the iShares 7-10 Year Treasury Bond ETF (NASDAQ:IEF), which I covered on numerous occasions last year. It's an unusual time for U.S. Treasuries, where market psychology and narrative seem to dominate analysis. This provides a perfect opportunity to identify value, which is exactly what I aimed to do with this analysis.

Last time around, I communicated a positive outlook on IEF, citing its lucrative risk premium. Upon reviewal, I have decided to stick with my outlook; here's why.

IEF's Recent Performance and Portfolio Review

The iShares 7-10 Year Treasury Bond ETF has delivered 8.06% in total return over the past year, largely driven by downward levelling of the U.S. Treasury yield curve and elevated distribution yields. The vehicle maintains an expense ratio of 0.15%, a monthly income-based distribution structure, and possesses a 30-day SEC yield of 3.97%. In addition, IEF ETF's current weighted average maturity stands at 8.46 years, effective duration 6.96 years, and convexity 58 basis points, illustrating exposure to the belly of the curve with mild secondary-affect gain prospects. In terms of its total risk premium, IEF has a weighted average yield-to-maturity of 4.14%.

Statistical Analysis

I tested a butterfly spread this morning (long 2-year yield, long 20-year yield, short 10-year yield), resembling equal long positions in the 2 and 10-year yield, and a short position in the 10-year yield. The butterfly isn't a trade set-up, as it ignores duration-adjusted position sizing and convexity biases. Instead, it provides a sense of which parts of the curve are statistically rich or cheap. Based on my test, the butterfly has a spread of negative 3.1%, suggesting the belly of the curve (10Y) provides a superior premium to a combination of the 2-year and 20-year.

I took the analysis a step further by observing z-scores. First. I'd like to cite the 10-2 spread widening to ~64 basis points on January 23rd, up from 36 basis points the same time last year and the 50 basis points witnessed 6-months ago. I smoothed the spread by running a 52-week rolling spread and subsequently transformed the value into a z-score. As shown by Figure 3, the 10-2 z-score is 1.01 sigma rich and the 20-10 z-score is around 0.377 sigma rich (sigma poor from the 10-year yield's perspective). The illustration suggests statistical relative value is present in the 10-year yield, especially relative to the front-end – a macro discussion is conveyed, later in the article, to support the claim.

Furthermore, I tested the half-lives of each spread and embedded my findings in figure 5. The blank spots on the line chart reveals periods where the drift term was undetected. In simple terms, it reveals periods where mean-reversion wasn't in play. That set aside, mean-reversion has shown consistent representation with the half life for the 10-2 being 6-weeks and the half life for the 20-10 being 2 weeks (according to my tests).

Lastly, I present figure 5, which isolated spread volatility. The spread volatility is important to note, as highly volatile spreads signal noise, whereas stable volatility, provides cleaner mean-reversion opportunities. Per my test, the spread volatility cooled throughout last year, largely due to the MOVE Index cooling. Secondarily, mean-reversion has already occurred (shown by the z-stats); in my experience, with a polynomial distribution, the volatility of mean-reversion reduces nearer to the moving average.

The conclusions I've made from the tests are:

  • The 10-year yield statistical relative value versus the 2-year yield, relative to periods post-2020.
  • The 20-year shows statistical value versus the 10, but now enough to suggest a dislocation (I deem 0.377 sigma is quite small).
  • Combined, the belly of the curve seems highly appealing, at this stage. The longer-end might be appealing to some, but I'd personally like seeing a more substantial premium before engaging.

Macro & Market Microstructure

Disinflation and a Lower Inflation Premium

I believe the U.S.' disinflation trend has consolidated. Before I discuss my outlook, I'd like to share external data. As illustrated by figure 6, the Cleveland Federal Reserve's nowcasting deems CPI at 2.35% for January. If realised, CPI would show a clear downtrend versus December's 2.7% and October's 3%.

The inflation term structure has shown a dramatic drop at the short-end, against the backdrop of CPI settling at-or-below estimates in recent months. For example, the one-year horizon has shifted down by 60 basis points month-over-month to 2.6%, back to pre-liberation day levels. Concurrently, the inflation premium has consolidated at 40 basis points, since August last year, suggesting inflation re-anchoring. The breakeven rate has ticked up to 232 basis points, after retreating to 220 basis point level, but I think this might be due to the bond market's over-reaction to monetary policy; we'll likely see breakeven come down if January CPI settles in-line.

The final set of 'direct data' I'd like to point out, is the Fed futures market, which expects one-to-two target rate cuts for the year. I wouldn't rely on the futures market too much at this stage, seeing as speculation of a new Federal Reserve Chair and a renewed wave of tariff threats have sent markets into a seemingly speculative environment.

Now that we've had a look at directly related to inflation, I'd like to share what I think about inflation and IEF's prospects leading forward.

I believe CPI will settle slightly below the market's estimate, in the low 2% environment (PCE might settle even lower, due to its substitution-effect). My base case stems from the fact that the unemployment rate of 4.4% has exceeded the natural rate of unemployment (~4.3%), consumer confidence remains subdued, and that inflation is non-monotonic, in other words, it reverts to mean. A counter-argument might be the output-gap, which has continued to surge, reaching 2.2% of GDP in Q3, 2025, likely due to productivity gains from AI and expansionary monetary policy. That being said, I'd like to think that the labour market will translate into a softer output gap in 2026 – I'd also highlight the fact that the output gains via AI, has partly been linked to capital investment, rather than a pure change in the production function – investment might show signs of slowing.

If realised, a lower inflation rate would likely drop the yield curve's level, giving IEF price support via lower duration. Of course, lower coupons will emerge in due course. However, the effect would likely be lagged, as IEF would need to reshuffle its asset base by a considerable amount before its lower coupon base would cancel out price gains. Some might argue that steepening will occur if additional rate cuts emerge – I'd highlight three things: Firstly, the U.S. is already 175 basis points in rate-cuts deep, secondly, the curve has steepened by a tremendous amount last year (we might see some levelling due to basic interest rates and curve dynamics), and third, we could see post-2026 tightening being priced into the curve at some stage this year (on the basis of potential re-inflation post 2026/27).

Herewith is a diagram illustrating the yield curve and its movement over the last twelve months. I'll use it as a reference for points made, later in the analysis.

Productivity Growth Expectations Might Cool

The U.S. yield curve has remained high for a few years. Although inflation and a fiscal risk premium can be attributed to the curve's level (especially at the longer end), I think many have forgotten that productivity growth also boosts yields. In Citigroup's (C) view and my own, productivity has gained due to the pandemic, due to higher total factor productivity (technology) and growth in capital investment. However, with government debt levels running high and marginal gains of tech-based productivity growth likely to diminish in-time, I won't be surprised seeing productivity gains slow in the near future, leading to lower mid-to-longer term yields.

Fiscal Risk Premium Consolidation

The U.S. fiscal risk premium is a big talking point, given the shift in political delivery from Washington. I think the uncertainty of the fiscal risk premium is probably the primary reason why I still prefer the belly of the curve, as opposed to the very-longer end. The market has somewhat normalised Washington's stance and USD-debasement has likely being price-in to a great extent. I believe investors might consolidating the fiscal risk premium at the 10-year, but remain coy about its very-long term influence.

Read more about the fiscal risk premium, via this link.

Lower Volatility and A Temporary Rise In Demand For Immediacy

Treasury volatility cooled significantly in 2025, allowing IEF to deliver a 1-year total return of 8.06% and the U.S. 10-year yield to drop by 43 basis points year-over-year. We might see continuation in this trend, given that inflation is re-anchoring. That being said, U.S. 10-year yields have spiked by 21 basis points in the past three months, on the basis of Fed Chair speculation and U.S. allies developing distrust in their relationship with Washington. In my opinion, this relationship forms an excellent buying opportunity, where a lower volatility regime meets a temporary rise in the demand for immediacy (investors can buy in a lower vol regime, while providing immediate liquidity to panic sellers and adversaries).

The 60/40 Portfolio's Risk Premia

AQR Capital Management was recently quoted saying: "The expected real return of a global 60/40 portfolio is 3.4%, around 1.5% higher than the all-time low reached in 2021, but still well below the long-term U.S. average of nearly 5% since 1900."

Why did I mention the above? Well, it shows that normalisation might be occurring, whereby the premia available via the 60/40 is starting to make sense again. In my view, it might be a good idea opting for Treasuries at this stage, given a return of demand for immediacy. In tandem, I'm in favour of maintaining equity exposure, but would use this period to hoard bonds, while waiting for demand for immediacy to return to the stock market, before I buy again.

Important Side Note: IEF is a duration-targeted ETF, whereby it opts to maintain a duration between 7-10 years. The ETFs premia will differ greatly from an investor who keeps a held-to-maturity bond. In fact, there's a fair argument that ETFs such as IEF ETF are to be treated as cyclical instruments, as opposed to long-term investment vehicles.

Risk Factors

Supply-Growth

The U.S. debt supply has grown substantially since the pro-COVID great financial liberation era, and even more-so after the pandemic. I think we could see supply being constrained soon, on the basis of bond holders casting doubt over interest costs, fiscal dominance, and the U.S.' credibility as a ballast. That being said, Washington seems quite unhinged about fiscal deficits at this stage, shifting focus to trade flows. A continued increase in the supply of debt, could drag 7-10-year yields up even further.

As a trade-off to the above, the U.S. Federal Reserve has stopped its quantitative tightening program. We might see balance sheet expansion in the coming years, including acquisitions of longer-term US. Treasuries. However, this in itself, doesn't guarantee higher net demand for U.S. Treasuries.

Ongoing Fiscal Risk

This sub-section somewhat relates to what I mentioned in the previous section. The "sell the U.S. trade" remains a material threat. Obviously, we'll need to see the narrative translate into action, which could be constrained due to two reasons: 1) Fed markets have the depth of U.S. markets, making full repatriation difficult, 2) Excess selling (by foreign holders) would probably result in losses for bond holders, which would not sit well with pension fund and insurance regulators. Nonetheless, a gradual shift out of U.S.-conviction portfolios (by Sovereign Wealth Funds, Governments, and Pension funds) could take shape.

FX Risk

The USD-debasement narrative has added headwinds to the USD and might translate into headwinds for U.S. Treasuries. The reliance on gold reserves and a shift to less USD-centric FX portfolios is well underway. Moreover, the U.S. has been in an expansionary monetary cycle, for the past two years. In addition, carry on emerging market FX has improved, and therefore lower demand for USD has occurred via that angle. The USD Index (DXY) currently trades ~2.81 standard deviations below its 12-month moving average. Lower appetite for the USD might translate into lower appetite for 7-10 year U.S. Treasuries and IEF ETF alike.

The Carry-Trade (Japan)

The U.S.-Japan carry trade, which includes purchases of U.S. Treasuries via funding from Japan has faced pressure lately, after Japan exited its negative rate regime. According to Desmond Lachman, the size of the carry trade is around $500 billion in notional, depending on time measured and methodology. Other sources, such as the Financial times have suggested size is usually half of that at $250 billion - excluding off-balance sheet items. Unwinding of the strategy could severely impact markets. That being said, I believe investors will migrate to other methods of funding. For example, we might see more investors fund through short-term repo markets. Sure, at lower profitability, but pressure to generate alpha remains, and portability needs to be considered.

IEF Can Suffer from left-tail risk

IEF has suffered from double-digit max drawdowns, even before the use case of U.S. Treasuries as safe-haven assets had been called into question. The ETF also possesses a 5% monthly value-at-risk of 2.82%, which I deem noteworthy, for a sovereign bond vehicle. In other words, isolation against market-based tail risk isn't guaranteed.

Conclusion

I'm re-iterating my bullish outlook on IEF ETF, which I'll likely sustain until its z-score relative to other parts of the curve settles down or if 10-year yields revert to the 400 basis point level. I acknowledge risks such as the USD debasement trade, the Japan carry trade's fragility, and fiscal dominance. However, I also believe the market has priced such factors into the yield already and that capital repatriation might face obstacles. I expect moderate cooling in 10-year yields this year and believe IEF ETF provides a solid total return opportunity.

data.title: Harmony Biosciences: Still Undervalued But Unappealing Risk/Reward (Downgrade)
data.description:
Article Thesis

I wrote about Harmony Biosciences (HRMY) in March last year and assigned it a “Buy” rating. My strategy for small biotech companies is to go with small positions (between ¼ and ½ initial position) for about 10 names, due to very high risks associated, especially with single-product companies. In this way, even if one or two or of them crashed, or even if one of them went bankrupt, my portfolio would not be much affected.

I generally go with ~¼ position for single-product companies and with ~1/2 for more diversified ones. For Harmony, I went with ~0.33x position, with one blockbuster product, and several others in their pipeline.

However, important things happened in the meantime. One of their pipeline candidates failed. Pressure on US drug prices intensified, and Harmony just holds a license from Bioprojet to manufacture and commercialize Wakix specifically in the US.

Therefore, a reassessment of their business, fair value, short-term outlook is in order, but also in the context of a broader biotech portfolio.

Business Analysis

Like mentioned in the introduction, Harmony holds the license to commercialize Wakix (pitolisant) in the US for narcolepsy, and this is their only commercial product. Wakix is indeed a superior product, being the only non-scheduled treatment for daytime sleepiness, with the company confident that this is an opportunity for “$1B+ in narcolepsy alone”. Besides, the company is trying to expand its market with two new formulas: Pitolisant GR (gastro-resistant) and Pitolisant HD (High Dose):

There are, however, some important caveats, besides the pressure on US drug prices mentioned in the introduction:

  • Pitolisant GR will obviously have a cannibalization effect, mostly taking market from Wakix, but with the potential to increase the number of patients, indeed.
  • A generic version of Wakix is estimated for 2031. Of course, Pitolisant GR’s main purpose is to extend the Pitolisant franchise to the mid-2040s, but it’s likely that a generic version of the initial Pitolisant will take market share from 2031.
  • Pitolisant HD’s main purpose is to expand the Pitolisant patient base into new indications, like IH (idiopathic hypersomnia). However, FDA refused last year their application of Pitolisant for IH, and it’s unclear if the new dosage can make the difference.

Then, let’s take a look at their pipeline:

With orphan drug designation status, I see Pitolisant for PWS (Prader Willi Syndrome) with more chances than the shaky Pitolisant HD for IH.

But the most important to notice is that ZYN-002, which was in March a candidate in an advanced stage for FXS (Fragile X Syndrome) and 22q (Deletion Syndrome), is withdrawn from their pipeline for both indications after its failure for FXS.

And we have EPX-100 as the more important candidate in their pipeline, with its two indications. While I do see more chances than for ZYN-002, there is a similar risk: a potential failure for one indication could trigger discontinuation for both.

Fair Value

With about $868M revenue for 2025 and about 8500 Wakix patients, that suggests ~$100K annual costs for Wakix, which, in my non-professional opinion, is a high cost:

My future revenue estimates are based on the following assumptions:

  • Pitolisant: the number of patients reaching 15K in 2030, but annual price decreasing to $40K due to US drug price pressure but also due to the launch of Pitolisant GR and HD, for $600M sales; then, decreasing from $300M in 2031 (generic launch) to $100M in 2034.
  • Pitolisant GR: 5K patients in 2030 (on top of the 15K for Pitolisant) x $50K annual price = $250M revenue; then, relatively constant until 2034 due to a Pitolisant generic launch.
  • Pitolisant PWS: launch in 2027 for a market of $600M and growing. However, with just a part if the market for Harmony related to excessive daytime sleepiness and behavioral symptoms, and with ~75% success probability, I model $100M peak sales in 2034.
  • Pitolisant HD: with ~$0.45B US estimated market for IH in 2033, but a low probability of success after the initial failure of Pitolisant, and ~$0.6B US estimated market for DM1, I model $150M peak sales: $50M for narcolepsy, $50M for IH, $50M for DM1.
  • EPX-100 for DS (Dravet Syndrome) and LGS (Lenox-Gastaut Syndrome): the company sees $1B-$2B potential peak sales for each of the two, but with potential payments to Epygenix of up to $130 million based on development and regulatory milestones, and up to $515 million if certain sales milestones are achieved, with other new drugs under development and with some probability factor, I model only ~$150M revenue for each in 2034.

Switching to profitability, I model:

  • A constant ~78% gross margin.
  • R&D margin naturally peaking at ~13% in 2027 (prior to new product launches), then 10% in 2029, then a modest 2% R&D increase to assure continuity with their other products.
  • S&M margin naturally peaking at ~20% in 2029 (2 years after new product launches), followed by a constant 5%-10% SM&GA annual decrease.

That would give about 37% EBIT margin in 2034, which I find a bit conservative for a biotech company, so I am comfortable with that.

Finally, for a DCF (Discounted Cash Flow) model, I model 0% growth in perpetuity (usual for a small biotech company) and 11.9% WACC (Weighted Average Cost of Capital), coming from 12.25% cost of equity and 6.75% cost of debt. I use a higher cost of equity than in my article from March (11.5%) due to higher risks (more about risks later).

Resulting Fair Value is $48, about 30% potential upside from the current price ($36.82). For a sensitivity analysis, let’s further vary WACC and growth in perpetuity:

Therefore, the stock seems at least fairly valued even with 13% WACC and -2% growth in perpetuity. However, we should notice that this is not a growth stock. I modeled a 7.2% revenue CAGR through 2030, which is already kind of modest, and then decreasing revenues. In this case, I see 30% unappealing as a margin of safety for a new position. To decide about my existing position, I need to look at risks in more detail.

Risks

I already mentioned about the pressure on US drug prices, and I consider ~$100K/year as a high cost for Wakix. I already modeled the price halved in 2030 but, if we look at what happened with GLP-1 drugs, it can be even worse.

Pipeline obviously comes with risks (with their history, we could say even higher than usual). While it was a little easier to model higher chances of success for Pitolisant for PWS and lower for Pitolisant HD for IH, the big question is about EPX-100. Yes, I modeled a probability factor, but if they failed with this one too, then Fair Value would be $33.6, leaving the stock slightly overvalued.

Then after the failure of ZYN-002, they are 100% reliant on a single product, Pitolisant, which is always a risk. And we see one more time why EPX-100 is so important.

I increased my cost of equity from 11.5% to 12.25%, about 0.25% for each risk mentioned above. Finally, I have slightly lower estimates than consensus for the next few years (maybe they don’t fear price pressures), about the same estimate for 2030 (pre-patent expiration), but then significantly higher estimates for terminal years:

I can’t understand very well the reasons for only $561M in 2034, they either assigned zero chances to EPX-100, or they assumed negligible sales from Pitolisant for narcolepsy after patent expiration. Since there is such a high discrepancy, I acknowledge this risk, but I can’t quantify it.

Comparative Reward/Risk For Other Biotech Companies

Like mentioned in the introduction, one of the main reasons for a position in Harmony was to have a diversified portfolio of about 10 names (strictly speaking about the biotech exposure, which is, overall, only a small part of my entire portfolio). However, in March I saw Harmony with 50%+ undervaluation and now with only 30%, and I also have other names now which I consider more interesting:

  • Harrow (HROW): shares, about 0.6x position.
  • Amphastar Pharmaceuticals (AMPH): shares and short naked (partially covered) Put, about 0.55x position.
  • Eton Pharmaceuticals (ETON): shares and Call Spread, about 0.5x position.
  • CorMedix (CRMD): shares, about 0.25x position.
  • Aquestive Therapeutics (AQST): shares, about 0.15x position.
  • ARS Pharmaceuticals (SPRY): Covered Call, about 0.3x position.
  • LENZ Therapeutics (LENZ): Short naked (partially covered) Put, about 0.2x position.
  • BioMarin Pharmaceuticals (BMRN): Call Spread, about 0.35x position; in my personal portfolio, without an article.
  • CRISPR Therapeutics (CRSP): shares, about 0.3x position; in my personal portfolio, without an article.
  • Viridian Therapeutics (VRDN): shares, about 0.25x position; in my personal portfolio, without an article.

With about the same risk on average, all the above have much higher long-term growth prospects, with one exception: AMPH, which is mostly focused on generics but expanding their proprietary drugs too. That’s why I have a short naked Put position in AMPH, because I can’t see very high growth. CRMD also has short-term declining sales after a bleak forecast, but I still see at least some long-term growth, and a much higher undervaluation than for HRMY. I hope to come back with articles about AMPH and CRMD soon.

If we turn to valuation, I see for all the above a higher undervaluation than for HRMY, maybe with one exception: VRDN. One of my strategies is to buy stocks in small biotech companies with a product in a very advance phase, with high chances of approval, and to sell it post-approval, and VRDN falls into this category, although I do consider selling it before approval, due to a nice quick profit in about 4 months and to minimize the risk.

With a portfolio of 10 names where I see much more interesting Reward/Risk, this is why I closed my position in Harmony last week at $36.4, like I mentioned in a comment, for just ~3% return since the initiation in March.

But why not switching to a short naked (partially covered) Put position like I have for AMPH or for LENZ? For AMPH there is a much lower fundamental risk with a lot of diversification. For LENZ, there is a much higher premium, I sold 15 Puts Jul’26 for $239, covered with cash for a drop to $9.35 (~$19.5 price today), meaning $239 / ($565-$239) = 73% maximum ROI in ~5 months.

For HRMY, if I sell a 30 Put Jul’26 for an estimated $290, and I cover it with cash for a drop to $18, maximum ROI would be $290 / ($1200-$290) = 32%. Huge difference!

Takeaway

Pipeline failures, especially ZYN-002, leave HRMY 100% reliant on Pitolisant. Further setbacks, notably with EPX-100, could increase that risk. Elsewhere, I see Pitolisant for PWS with higher chances than Pitolisant HD for IH, due to a past failure of the existing Pitolisant for IH.

With new products, but pressure on US drug prices, I modeled revenue CAGR of 7.2% through 2030, followed by declining sales post-2031 as a Pitolisant generic could be launched.

Harmony now offers only modest upside, with a DCF-derived fair value estimate of $48 versus a current price of $36.82.

Other portfolio biotech holdings offer higher growth and higher undervaluation. Harmony's risk/reward is less compelling, for shares or for a short Put position, leading to the decision to exit in favor of better opportunities.
